pinterest.title: Lamb Shanks in Red Mole Sauce — Halal Braised Lamb Recipe
pinterest.description: This lamb shank recipe crosses two culinary traditions: French braising technique meets a from-scratch red mole built on chipotle, pasilla, roasted tomato, and Mexican chocolate. Low heat, long time, serious depth of flavor. Halal, unhurried, and worth every minute. Full recipe at the link.

instagram.caption:
Some dishes ask nothing of you. This one asks for your afternoon.

Red mole is not a sauce you stumble into. You toast the chiles — pasilla, chipotle — until the kitchen smells like smoke and something older than any recipe you own. You roast the tomatoes until they collapse. You melt in the Mexican chocolate and wonder, briefly, if this is too much. It is not too much.

The lamb shanks go in after a proper sear, buried under all of it, and the oven does the slow, patient work that braising was invented for. French technique, Mexican soul, a halal table that refuses to settle for less than the best of both.

When it comes out — meat pulling from the bone, sauce the color of dark earth — you understand why some recipes take all day. They are not wasting your time. They are teaching you something about it.

Recipe is on the blog. Link in bio.

email.subject: Lamb Shanks + Red Mole: Try This
email.preheader: Chipotle, pasilla, Mexican chocolate, and a French braise. This one earns its time.
email.body_html:
There is a version of fusion food that is lazy — two cuisines thrown together because someone thought it sounded interesting. This is not that.

The new recipe on the blog is lamb shanks braised in red mole sauce, and it works because the logic holds all the way through. French braising gives the technique: the hard sear, the low oven, the long time, the collagen surrendering slowly into the sauce. Mexican mole gives the character: toasted pasilla and chipotle chiles, roasted tomatoes, garlic cooked until it sweetens, and Mexican chocolate that rounds every sharp edge without making anything coy or sweet.

Together they produce something that neither tradition would call foreign. The French cook recognizes the braise. The Mexican cook recognizes the mole. Everyone at the table recognizes that it is very good.

The process takes time. Not complicated time — you are not performing surgery — but patient time. Toast the chiles. Roast the tomatoes. Build the sauce in layers. Sear the shanks until the crust is real. Then the oven handles the rest, and you can read a book or stand near the kitchen and feel quietly accomplished.

It is halal, it is serious, and it is the kind of dish that makes a Sunday feel like it was used well.

Full recipe, technique notes, and a few things worth knowing about working with dried chiles:

Read: Lamb Shanks in Red Mole Sauce

linkedin.body:
Most fusion cooking fails at the level of logic. Two cuisines meet on a plate because someone thought the contrast was interesting, not because the techniques actually reinforce each other.

The recipe I published this week tries to earn its combination. Lamb shanks braised in red mole sauce — French structure, Mexican depth.

French braising is fundamentally about patience and chemistry: you sear for crust and Maillard flavor, then surrender the meat to low, wet heat over several hours while collagen converts to gelatin and the braise becomes something unctuous and coherent. The technique is indifferent to what liquid surrounds the meat. It works with wine. It works with stock. It works, as it turns out, with a carefully built red mole.

Mole is also a study in patience. Dried chiles — pasilla, chipotle — toasted until fragrant, then rehydrated. Tomatoes roasted until their water burns off and their sugars concentrate. Mexican chocolate added late, not for sweetness but for body and a faint bitterness that keeps the sauce from being cloying.

What the two traditions share is a refusal to rush. Neither technique respects shortcuts. Both reward attention.

I think that is why the dish works: not because Mexico and France are exotic neighbors, but because the underlying discipline is the same. Cook slowly. Build in layers. Let time do the work you cannot do yourself.

The full recipe is on the blog if you want the details.

tweets: I made lamb shanks in red mole sauce this week. French braise, Mexican chile sauce, Mexican chocolate. It should not work this well. It absolutely works.

Mole starts with toasted dried chiles — pasilla and chipotle. You toast them dry in a pan until the smell changes. Smoke, heat, something vegetal underneath. Then they rehydrate and become the base of something serious.

The French side of this is straightforward: hard sear on the shanks, build the mole around them, low oven for 2.5-3 hours. Braising is indifferent to what liquid you use. Collagen becomes gelatin regardless. The mole just makes it better.

Mexican chocolate goes in near the end. Not for sweetness. For body, slight bitterness, and the thing that makes the sauce taste finished rather than assembled. This is not a dessert move. It is a mole move.
The full lamb shank recipe — technique notes and all — is on the blog: https://mubashirr.com/lamb-shanks-red-mole-sauce-halal-french-cooking/

facebook.body:
New recipe is up: lamb shanks braised in red mole sauce.

The mole is built from scratch — toasted pasilla and chipotle chiles, roasted tomatoes, Mexican chocolate. The technique is French: long braise, low heat, the kind of patience that turns a tough cut into something that pulls apart without being asked.

It takes most of an afternoon. It is worth most of an afternoon.

Halal, serious, and one of the better things to come out of my kitchen in a while. Recipe at the link.
